message.content:
You are working in /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster (a bash/Rust infrastructure repo). Perform a mechanical code sweep: convert the last skopeo consumer to gcrane, then purge skopeo from the build-tool cohort and its entire test/spec surface. Edit files in place. Do NOT run git commit, do NOT run any cloud/live operation, do NOT run test suites. NEVER use `cd` in bash commands — absolute paths only. Never pipe tabtarget invocations.

## Part 1 — Convert the bind mirror from `skopeo copy --all` to `gcrane cp`

- `Tools/rbk/rbgjm/rbgjm01-mirror-image.sh` is the Cloud Build step body that does the copy. Replace its skopeo invocation with `gcrane cp` (registry-to-registry multi-platform copy; gcrane cp copies all platforms of an index by default — no --all flag needed; it fails on a missing source, matching bind's pin-exactly semantic).
- gcrane auths Google registries ambiently via the GCE metadata server (google.Keychain matches *.pkg.dev) — NO token fetch, NO `crane auth login`, NO docker login is needed for the GAR destination side. If the current step body fetches an OAuth token (look for a `#@rbgjs_include rbgjs-token-fetch` marker or inline curl to the metadata server for registry auth), remove that machinery. CAUTION: the bind SOURCE is an upstream public registry (RBRV_BIND_IMAGE / _RBGA_BIND_SOURCE) — public pulls need no auth either.
- Builder image: in `Tools/rbk/rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh`, `zrbfd_mirror_submit` composes the step with `--arg name "${z_rbfc_tool_skopeo}"` and a hardcoded `printf '#!/bin/bash\n%s'` shebang. The mirror is a sealed-reliquary consumer (the vessel supplies RBRV_RELIQUARY), so it must ride the PINNED reliquary gcrane: use `${z_rbfc_tool_gcrane}` (already provided by the resolver in `Tools/rbk/rbfca_StepAssembly.sh` — confirm the variable name there). The gcrane builder image is distroless `:debug` whose only shell is `/busybox/sh` — change the composed shebang for this step to `#!/busybox/sh` and make sure the step body is POSIX sh (no bashisms). Look at how `Tools/rbk/rblds_Spine.sh` handles the `busybox` entrypoint and how the bole capture step `Tools/rbk/rbgjl/rbgjl01-ensconce-capture.sh` is written for gcrane — match those conventions, including any `#@rbgjs_include rbgjs-gcrane-*` snippet usage if appropriate (snippets live in `Tools/rbk/rbgjs/`; the include expander is `zrbfc_expand_includes`, used by mirror submit via `zrbfc_write_script_body` — check whether mirror submit expands includes at all before relying on a snippet; if it does not, inline instead).
- Update the step's header comment (it documents substitutions consumed) and the step id if it names skopeo.

## Part 2 — Drop skopeo from the cohort and all plumbing

- Conclave cohort MANIFEST: find the tool-cohort manifest list (grep skopeo in `Tools/rbk/rbldr_Reliquary.sh`, `Tools/rbk/rbgjl/rbgjl03-conclave-capture.sh`, and `Tools/rbk/rbgc_Constants.sh`). Remove the skopeo member. The constant is `RBGC_RELIQUARY_TOOL_SKOPEO` — remove it and every reference.
- `z_rbfc_tool_skopeo` resolver plumbing in `Tools/rbk/rbfca_StepAssembly.sh` (and any leaked global declaration in `Tools/rbk/rbfc0_FoundryCore.sh`) — remove.
- Reliquary preflight check `Tools/rbk/rbgjr/rbgjr01-reliquary-preflight.sh` — remove the skopeo presence check.
- Help/count strings mentioning skopeo or the tool count (e.g. "six builder tool images" style phrases) — `Tools/rbk/rbfly_Yoke.sh` counts are supposedly derived; verify they derive and fix any literal that doesn't. Also check `Tools/rbk/rbfln_Inventory.sh`, `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhodf_director_first_build.sh` (it says "six builder tool images (gcloud, docker, alpine, syft, binfmt, skopeo)") — update the list and count there to the post-eviction cohort (confirm actual remaining members from the manifest you edited; gcrane is a member).
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs`: the reliquary-lifecycle fixture asserts the cohort CONTAINS `rbi_skopeo` via const `RBTDRC_RELIQUARY_TAG_SKOPEO`. Remove the const and the assertion (the fixture must remain meaningful for the remaining members).
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdru_cupel.rs`: remove "skopeo" from the `ZRBTDRU_GCB_ALLOWED` allowlist.
- `Tools/rbk/rbgjs/rbgjs-token-fetch.sh`: after the mirror conversion, grep `#@rbgjs_include rbgjs-token-fetch` across Tools/ — if zero callers remain, delete the file AND remove its row from the snippet table in `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/CBG-CloudBuildGuide.md` (note: CBG path may be `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/CBG-CloudBuildGuide.md` — find it). Same check for `Tools/rbk/rbgjs/rbgjs-skopeo-fingerprint.sh` if it still exists — if caller-less, delete it and its CBG row too.
- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc`: posture claims that anticipate full skopeo eviction ("the last skopeo holdout" style prose) go false once this lands — update them to state the eviction is complete. KEEP the rejected-credential-helper memo content and reference links as history.
- `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhodb_director_bind.sh`: the operator handbook narrates "Cloud Build runs skopeo to copy" and "skopeo copy plus about/vouch metadata" — update the narrative to gcrane.
- Bind/mirror spec prose: grep skopeo in `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/*.adoc` — any spec describing the mirror pipeline step (likely RBSAP-ark_plumb.adoc or a bind-specific doc, also RBSCJ-CloudBuildJson.adoc and RBSAB) needs the skopeo→gcrane update. Use judgment: operational descriptions must be updated; historical/decision-record prose (e.g. "skopeo was ruled out because...") stays.

## Part 3 — Full census

Finish with `grep -rn skopeo /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/tt` (case-insensitive too) and classify EVERY remaining hit as: (a) fixed by you, (b) deliberately kept history (memos under Memos/, scar/decision-record prose in specs, CHANGELOG-like content), or (c) unsure. Do not leave operational code, test, spec-contract, or handbook references unfixed.

## Verification (static only)

- Run `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/tt/rbw-tl.Shellcheck.sh` — must pass.
- Run `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh` — theurge crate must compile (your .rs edits).
- Run `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/tt/rbw-tt.Test.sh` — theurge unit tests must pass.
- Do NOT run rbw-ts suites (they gate on a clean tree and the tree is dirty with your edits).

## Report back

Return: (1) the full list of files changed with one line each on what changed; (2) the Part 3 census classification table of every remaining skopeo hit; (3) any place where you were unsure or deviated from these instructions (especially the shebang/builder/auth details of the mirror step); (4) verification results. Be precise — your output will be reviewed line-by-line.

- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

They contain compressed root filesystem tarballs for WSL environments or CoreOS disk images for standard virtualization platforms. These artifacts use non-standard media types, rely on custom annotations such as disktype to indicate their intended use, and carry opaque blobs rather than composable filesystem layers. While they are platform-indexed in the OCI sense, they are not platform-runnable as containers would be. Because of these differences, standard `podman pull` and `docker pull` cannot correctly retrieve and reassemble VM artifacts — they operate at the container-runtime abstraction level, which assumes runnable images with standard layer structure. Tools like crane (manifest and blob-level registry operations), skopeo (cross-registry copying with format awareness), and oras (OCI Registry As Storage, designed for non-container artifacts) work below that abstraction at the registry API and manifest level, which is why they can handle VM artifacts correctly.

content.content:
70	
71	**Cloud Build logging.**
72	Build requests set `options.logging: CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY`.
73	Log hygiene (no credential echo) was audited during trigger migration script inlining.
74	
75	**Registry authentication is ambient and per-step, with no credential-helper dependency.**
76	Docker-based steps authenticate to GAR automatically — Cloud Build pre-populates Docker credentials, so no `docker login` step is needed.
77	The capture steps use **`gcrane`** (crane's Google-auth sibling — same `cp`/`manifest`/`tag` engine), whose `google.Keychain` matches `*.pkg.dev` and draws credentials from Application Default Credentials → the GCE metadata server.
78	On a Cloud Build worker the Mason SA *is* that metadata-server identity, so gcrane reads and writes GAR ambiently with **no token fetch, no `crane auth login`, no credential-helper image, and no new IAM grant**.
79	This is the same ambient model the docker-based steps already use, extended from docker to crane — one auth story across the capture family rather than two.
80	The one remaining tool that cannot auth ambiently is **skopeo** (the made-side bind mirror): skopeo cannot consume the gcloud credential helper — gcloud is not a single binary to place on the skopeo container's PATH — so each skopeo step fetches a short-lived OAuth2 token directly from the GCB metadata server (the Mason SA's ambient identity) and passes it via `--src-creds`/`--dest-creds`.
81	This is the canonical skopeo→Artifact Registry pattern, not a workaround — the credential-helper alternative was evaluated and rejected (see Deferred Items).
82	The token is held in step-local memory and re-minted by each step that needs it; it is never written to `/workspace`.
83	_Invariant: the {rbtgr_workspace} carries non-secret build data only — secrets stay step-local._
84	As skopeo is evicted across the crane-embrace cutovers, the per-step token fetch retires entirely and ambient gcrane auth becomes universal.
85	
86	**Provenance on demand.**
87	`rbfl_tally()` lists all hallmarks and queries Container Analysis inline for each per-platform image, displaying SLSA build level, build invocation ID, and single-origin verification per hallmark.
88	Tabtarget:
89	{rbtgo_hallmark_tally}.
90	
91	===== Pipeline Architecture: Single-Platform Vessels
92	
93	Five build steps plus CB-native `images:` push (binfmt step excluded at stitch time).
94	Applies to vessels whose `RBRV_CONJURE_PLATFORMS` matches `RBGC_BUILD_RUNNER_PLATFORM`.
95	
96	1. **derive-tag-base** — compute timestamps and tag components from CB substitutions; log build strategy
97	2. **build-and-load** — `docker buildx build --load` (single platform into local daemon)
98	3. **sbom** — Syft scan via `registry:` transport
99	4. **assemble-metadata** — per-platform `build_info.json` with SLSA summary fields
100	5. **build-and-push-metadata** — multi-platform `about` container pushed to `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/<hallmark>/about:<hallmark>` via `buildx --push`
101	
102	`images:` field declares the single per-platform URI → CB pushes and generates SLSA Level 3.
103	
104	===== Pipeline Architecture: Multi-Platform Vessels
105	
106	Seven build steps plus CB-native `images:` push (binfmt step included at stitch time).
107	Applies to vessels whose `RBRV_CONJURE_PLATFORMS` differs from `RBGC_BUILD_RUNNER_PLATFORM`.
108	About steps (`rbgja/`) are embedded in the build job via stitch (combined image+about).
109	Vouch (`rbgjv/`) runs as a separate Cloud Build job.
110	
111	1. **derive-tag-base** — compute timestamps and tag components; log build strategy
112	2. **qemu-binfmt** — register QEMU user-static for cross-arch emulation
113	3. **buildx-push-multi** — `docker buildx build --push` all platforms to intermediate `-multi` tag; capture `cache_before.json` (Docker daemon state pre-build) and `buildkit_metadata.json` (via `--metadata-file`)
114	4. **per-platform-pullback** — `docker pull --platform` each arch, tag individually
115	5. **push-per-platform** — `docker push` each per-platform tag (pre-push for imagetools); capture `cache_after.json` (Docker daemon state post-push)
116	6. **imagetools-create** — reassemble consumer-facing manifest list from per-platform tags

316	Deploy-time policy enforcement: only attested images can be deployed.

326	**skopeo credential helper — rejected, not merely deferred (evaluated 2026-06).**
327	The standalone `docker-credential-gcr` helper could let skopeo authenticate to GAR via a `credHelpers` config entry instead of the inline token fetch (see Current Posture).
328	Rejected: on GCB the helper mints the _same_ metadata-server token we already obtain (no new capability), but requires injecting a new binary into the skopeo container — a fresh vouched supply-chain dependency, the opposite of our minimize-the-dependency-manifold posture — and skopeo→GAR via credential helper carries documented 400-error friction (skopeo issue #2573, locked/unresolved), with the community fallback being the explicit `oauth2accesstoken` token, i.e. exactly the current approach.
329	Revisit only if skopeo gains first-class GAR/ADC authentication or the helper friction is resolved upstream.
330	Scope note: the capture steps no longer use skopeo at all — the crane-embrace moved them to gcrane, which auths GAR ambiently (see Current Posture) — so this rejection now governs only the remaining made-side skopeo (the bind mirror) until it too is evicted.
331	
332	**Capture-machinery duplication — token fetch, skopeo-capture, vouch-push.**
333	The cloud capture steps presently duplicate logic by copy: the metadata-token fetch across the remaining skopeo steps, the read→digest→fingerprint→copy block (the enshrine fork — bole now rides the shared `gcrane-fingerprint` snippet and gcrane's ambient auth), and the `FROM scratch` vouch-push step (Lode `rbgjl02` and hallmark-verify `rbgjv03`).
334	This is acknowledged, not accidental — Cloud Build offers no native step reuse (no YAML anchors or includes), and its blessed reuse mechanism, custom builder images, conflicts with the _cloud is just labor; the Director controls what enters_ posture above (a custom builder holding our logic would be a new vouched artifact rather than inline-from-repo).
335	Remedy (built in the capture-machinery-unification refactor): a host assembly spine (aligned with the `stitch` vocabulary), a shared cloud step-library (`rbgjs`) of compose-once snippets, and a dispatch-time recipe validator that derives each step's substitution coverage — composed once and referenced, replacing copy-by-example with compose-from-library. The `/workspace` inter-step channel stays guarded in-step (the steps' own `test -f` preconditions) rather than by a dispatch contract.
336	Dedup is deliberately partial: only the survivor (bole) rides the spine; the doomed enshrine and inscribe forks are left in place for later removal, so the duplicated copies above persist until that cutover.

71:.. NOTE: V2 DELETE-by-tag has producer-side asymmetric semantics. For single-platform pushes (`docker push`), the tag points to a single image manifest; DELETE removes it, the package becomes empty, and GAR drops the package container from `ListPackages`. For multi-platform pushes (`docker buildx --push`, `skopeo copy --all`), the tag points to an OCI image index referencing N per-platform child manifests; DELETE removes only the index, leaving the N children as untagged orphan content. The package container persists in `ListPackages` because it still holds those untagged child manifests.
---
34:VM images are OCI artifacts rather than standard container images. They contain compressed root filesystem tarballs for WSL environments or CoreOS disk images for standard virtualization platforms. These artifacts use non-standard media types, rely on custom annotations such as disktype to indicate their intended use, and carry opaque blobs rather than composable filesystem layers. While they are platform-indexed in the OCI sense, they are not platform-runnable as containers would be. Because of these differences, standard `podman pull` and `docker pull` cannot correctly retrieve and reassemble VM artifacts — they operate at the container-runtime abstraction level, which assumes runnable images with standard layer structure. Tools like crane (manifest and blob-level registry operations), skopeo (cross-registry copying with format awareness), and oras (OCI Registry As Storage, designed for non-container artifacts) work below that abstraction at the registry API and manifest level, which is why they can handle VM artifacts correctly.
36:The bootstrap process faces a chicken-and-egg problem. The mirroring workflow requires crane, oras, or skopeo, but Recipe Bottle does not want to obligate users to install and maintain these tools on their host systems. The solution is to use an ignite VM, initialized with the latest uncontrolled upstream image, as a disposable tooling environment. Crane and jq are installed inside the ignite VM, and all registry operations execute there. The ignite VM is discarded after completing the mirroring operation.

12	# member tags by gcrane tag (registry-side, no blob round-trip). Author the
13	# provenance envelope and stage it for step 02 (the :rbi_vouch artifact) and for

78	  # GAR write in the sense the spec requires (no host pre-submit window). EXISTING_SHA
79	  # is computed the same way as SHA (sha256sum of the raw manifest), so the compare is
80	  # apples-to-apples. A manifest-read failure is treated as absent: cloud-side
81	  # auth/network is reliable, and a genuine GAR outage fails the copy below regardless.
82	  EXISTING_RAW="/workspace/ensconce_existing_${SLOT}.json"
83	  if gcrane manifest "${PKG}:${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE}" > "${EXISTING_RAW}" 2>/dev/null; then
84	    EXISTING_SHA=$(sha256sum "${EXISTING_RAW}" | cut -d' ' -f1)
85	    if [ "${EXISTING_SHA}" = "${SHA}" ]; then
86	      echo "Touchmark ${STAMP} already holds identical digest sha256:${SHA} — idempotent retry, proceeding."
87	    else
88	      echo "FATAL: touchmark collision at ${PKG}" >&2
89	      echo "  existing :${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE} -> sha256:${EXISTING_SHA}" >&2
90	      echo "  refusing to clobber with  -> sha256:${SHA}" >&2
91	      echo "  (banish the Lode first, or ensconce under a fresh touchmark)" >&2
92	      exit 1
93	    fi
94	  else
95	    echo "Touchmark ${STAMP} is fresh (no existing :${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE})."
96	  fi
97	
98	  # Copy upstream into the Lode package under the canonical digest tag. gcrane cp
99	  # copies the whole reference (manifest list and all per-platform manifests for a
100	  # multi-arch index) and preserves the manifest digest byte-for-byte.
101	  gcrane cp "${ORIGIN}" "${PKG}:${DIGEST_TAG}" \
102	    || { echo "FATAL: gcrane cp failed for slot ${SLOT}" >&2; exit 1; }
103	
104	  # Apply remaining member tags with gcrane tag — a registry-side re-tag of the
105	  # already-copied manifest, no blob round-trip (replaces the GAR->GAR copy).
106	  for MEMBER_TAG in "${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE}" "${FINGERPRINT}"; do
107	    gcrane tag "${PKG}:${DIGEST_TAG}" "${MEMBER_TAG}" \
108	      || { echo "FATAL: gcrane tag ${MEMBER_TAG} failed for slot ${SLOT}" >&2; exit 1; }
109	  done
110	
111	  echo "Slot ${SLOT} ensconced: ${STAMP}"
112	
113	  # Author the provenance envelope (identical content lands in :rbi_vouch and the
114	  # host capture-file). No jq dependency — values are controlled (sanitized origin,
115	  # hex digest, SA email, build id, ISO timestamp); none can carry a literal quote.
116	  # rblv_members[] is the cardinality axis — length 1 for the bole singleton.
117	  ENVELOPE='{'
118	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_schema\":\"${_RBGL_VOUCH_SCHEMA}\","
119	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_kind\":\"bole\","
120	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_lode\":\"${STAMP}\","
121	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_acquired_at\":\"${ACQUIRED_AT}\","
122	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_acquired_by\":\"${_RBGL_ACQUIRED_BY}\","
123	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_capture_build\":\"${BUILD_ID:-}\","
124	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_trust_grade\":\"${_RBGL_TRUST_GRADE}\","
125	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_signature\":null,"
126	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_members\":[{"
127	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_name\":\"${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE}\","
128	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_origin\":\"${ORIGIN}\","
129	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_digest\":\"sha256:${SHA}\","
130	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_verification\":\"oci-digest\","
131	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"rblv_tags\":[\"${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE}\",\"${DIGEST_TAG}\",\"${FINGERPRINT}\"]"
132	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}}]}"
133	
134	  # Stage the envelope for step 02 (pushes it as the :rbi_vouch artifact).
135	  printf '%s' "${ENVELOPE}" > "/workspace/lode_${STAMP}_vouch.json"
136	  echo "${STAMP}" >> /workspace/lode_stamps.txt
137	
138	  # Accumulate host-facing result (the capture-file carries the same envelope).
139	  if [ "${FIRST}" = "true" ]; then
140	    FIRST=false
141	  else
142	    RESULT="${RESULT},"
143	  fi
144	  RESULT="${RESULT}\"rbls_slot_${SLOT}\":{\"rbls_stamp\":\"${STAMP}\",\"rbls_vouch\":${ENVELOPE}}"
145	done
146	
147	RESULT="${RESULT}}"
148	
149	echo "=== Writing capture results ==="
150	echo "${RESULT}"
151	
152	# Write to buildStepOutputs channel (host extracts per-Lode envelope -> capture-file)
153	mkdir -p /builder/outputs
154	printf '%s' "${RESULT}" > /builder/outputs/output
155	
156	echo "=== Ensconce capture step complete ==="

125	Structured output is JSON. **Use the interpreter's native serializer when it is
126	free; hand-roll only to avoid pulling a tool into the builder container.** Same
127	rule everywhere, opposite mechanic by environment:
128	
129	- **python**: `json.dump` / `json.loads` — stdlib, free. Always.
130	- **host**: `jq -n --arg/--slurpfile` — the host has `jq`; never concatenate,
131	  never YAML (Cloud Build's wire format is JSON; RBSCJ).
132	- **bash, inside a step**: hand-rolled `printf`/concatenation, because pulling
133	  `jq` into a minimal skopeo container is friction — and permitted *only* because
134	  every interpolated value is controlled (hex digests, ISO timestamps, sanitized
135	  origins, SA emails), none of which can carry a literal quote:
136	
137	```bash
138	# No jq inside the step — values are controlled; none can carry a quote.
139	ENVELOPE='{'
140	ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"digest\":\"sha256:${SHA}\","
141	ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"tags\":[\"${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE}\",\"${FINGERPRINT}\"]"
142	ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}}"
143	```
144	
145	If a value could ever carry a quote or newline, it is not controlled — do not
146	hand-roll it.
147	
148	Key naming rides ACG's wire-format clause: JSON this project authors on both
149	sides carries the format's minted key-sprue (e.g. `rblv_*` for the Lode vouch
150	envelope); JSON conforming to a foreign schema keeps the foreign keys. Rule and
151	conversion move: ACG "wire formats are named homes" / ACGm_108.
152	
153	### Separate pure logic from network ops
154	
155	Resolve inputs, build paths, and author envelopes in a block with no network
156	calls; isolate `skopeo`/`gcloud`/`curl`/`urllib` calls in their own block. The
157	pure block is testable without the cloud; the network block can be stubbed.
158	BCG's visible-transformation philosophy applied to a step's internal structure.
159	
160	### Step-body skeleton
161	
162	**bash/sh**: a single file; first executable line `set -euo pipefail`; no module
163	header, kindle, or sentinel (BUK constructs absent in the builder). The shebang
164	is stamped by the assembler, not written by the author (CBh_101).
165	
166	**python**: stdlib only — no `pip install` in-step; the preamble above; `main()`
167	guarded by `if __name__ == "__main__":`.
168	
169	### GAR authentication idiom
170	
171	GAR reads/writes use the in-memory metadata token as credentials, never a
172	credential helper (the gcloud helper is unavailable to skopeo; rationale in
173	RBSCB). The token itself is fetched fresh per step — bash via the `token-fetch`
174	snippet (CBh_101), python via `urllib` against `metadata.google.internal`. skopeo
175	copies pass `--all` for multi-arch safety:
176	
177	```bash
178	skopeo copy --all "docker://${ORIGIN}" "docker://${PKG}:${DIGEST_TAG}" \
179	  --dest-creds "oauth2accesstoken:${TOKEN}" \
180	  || { echo "FATAL: skopeo copy failed for ${ORIGIN}" >&2; exit 1; }
181	```
182	
183	The *invariant* that the token never reaches `/workspace` is CBi_102; this is the
184	*idiom* that honors it.
185

192	python, `CBh_` host-composition seam. Numbered from 101 to leave room for
193	insertions; once a rule has a citer it is never renumbered.

input.new_string:
### GAR authentication idiom

GAR reads/writes use gcrane's ambient `google.Keychain`, which draws credentials
from Application Default Credentials → the GCE metadata server (the Mason SA's
ambient identity on Cloud Build workers).
No token fetch, no `crane auth login`, no credential-helper image.
The same ambient model the docker-based steps use, extended from docker to crane — one auth story across all steps.

```bash
gcrane cp "${ORIGIN}" "${PKG}:${DIGEST_TAG}" \
  || { echo "FATAL: gcrane cp failed for ${ORIGIN}" >&2; exit 1; }
```

The *invariant* that credentials never reach `/workspace` is CBi_102; the ambient
model honors it structurally — no token is ever minted by the step body.
(Historical: skopeo used an in-memory metadata token via `--dest-creds oauth2accesstoken:${TOKEN}`;
that pattern is retired. Rationale for why a credential-helper was rejected is in RBSCB.)

/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc:34:VM images are OCI artifacts rather than standard container images. They contain compressed root filesystem tarballs for WSL environments or CoreOS disk images for standard virtualization platforms. These artifacts use non-standard media types, rely on custom annotations such as disktype to indicate their intended use, and carry opaque blobs rather than composable filesystem layers. While they are platform-indexed in the OCI sense, they are not platform-runnable as containers would be. Because of these differences, standard `podman pull` and `docker pull` cannot correctly retrieve and reassemble VM artifacts — they operate at the container-runtime abstraction level, which assumes runnable images with standard layer structure. Tools like crane (manifest and blob-level registry operations), skopeo (cross-registry copying with format awareness), and oras (OCI Registry As Storage, designed for non-container artifacts) work below that abstraction at the registry API and manifest level, which is why they can handle VM artifacts correctly.
/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc:36:The bootstrap process faces a chicken-and-egg problem. The mirroring workflow requires crane, oras, or skopeo, but Recipe Bottle does not want to obligate users to install and maintain these tools on their host systems. The solution is to use an ignite VM, initialized with the latest uncontrolled upstream image, as a disposable tooling environment. Crane and jq are installed inside the ignite VM, and all registry operations execute there. The ignite VM is discarded after completing the mirroring operation.

While they are platform-indexed in the OCI sense, they are not platform-runnable as containers would be. Because of these differences, standard `podman pull` and `docker pull` cannot correctly retrieve and reassemble VM artifacts — they operate at the container-runtime abstraction level, which assumes runnable images with standard layer structure. Tools like crane (manifest and blob-level registry operations), skopeo (cross-registry copying with format awareness), and oras (OCI Registry As Storage, designed for non-container artifacts) work below that abstraction at the registry API and manifest level, which is why they can handle VM artifacts correctly.

The solution is to use an ignite VM, initialized with the latest uncontrolled upstream image, as a disposable tooling environment.
